quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Atomic number (Z)"?

options.text: The number of protons in an atom's nucleus; uniquely identifies the element and determines its chemical properties
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: "Atomic number (Z)" is defined as: The number of protons in an atom's nucleus; uniquely identifies the element and determines its chemical properties. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Isotope"?

options.text: A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons; same Z but different A (e.g., ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C)
Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰)
A proton (H⁺) acceptor

quiz_questions.explanation: "Isotope" is defined as: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons; same Z but different A (e.g., ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mass number (A)"?

options.text: The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass
The total number of protons plus neutrons in the nucleus; A = Z + N
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mass number (A)" is defined as: The total number of protons plus neutrons in the nucleus; A = Z + N. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Atomic mass unit (amu)"?

options.text: Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it
ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
Defined as 1/12 the mass of a ¹²C atom; 1 amu = 1.661 × 10⁻²⁴ g
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: "Atomic mass unit (amu)" is defined as: Defined as 1/12 the mass of a ¹²C atom; 1 amu = 1.661 × 10⁻²⁴ g. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ion"?

options.text: A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole
An atom that has gained or lost electrons; cations are positive (lost e⁻), anions are negative (gained e⁻)
The equilibrium constant for the dissolution of a sparingly soluble ionic compound; Ksp = product of ion concentrations raised to stoichiometric powers
A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ion" is defined as: An atom that has gained or lost electrons; cations are positive (lost e⁻), anions are negative (gained e⁻). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The number of protons in an atom's nucleus; uniquely identifies the element and determines its chemical properties"?

options.text: Intermediate
Atomic number (Z)
Metallic bond
Buffer capacity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Atomic number (Z)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons; same Z but different A (e.g., ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C)"?

options.text: Isotope
Alloy
Fractional distillation
End point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Isotope".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The total number of protons plus neutrons in the nucleus; A = Z + N"?

options.text: Mass number (A)
Buffer capacity
Conjugate acid-base pair
Kp vs. Kc

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mass number (A)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Defined as 1/12 the mass of a ¹²C atom; 1 amu = 1.661 × 10⁻²⁴ g"?

options.text: Alloy
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
pOH
Atomic mass unit (amu)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Atomic mass unit (amu)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An atom that has gained or lost electrons; cations are positive (lost e⁻), anions are negative (gained e⁻)"?

options.text: Mole ratio
Ion
Nernst equation
Homogeneous catalyst

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles is accurate?

options.text: The autoionization of water (Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C) connects [H⁺] and [OH⁻].
Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
The relative strength of an acid is inversely related to the strength of its conjugate base.
Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles forms the foundation of chemistry.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.
Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and geometry.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: The atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons and defines the element.
Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) has a strong first dissociation and weak second (Ka2 = 0.012).
The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.
Green chemistry aims to maximize both percent yield and atom economy while minimizing waste and hazardous byproducts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons.
Strong acid–strong base titrations have equivalence point at pH 7.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.
Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: The Nernst equation adjusts E for non-standard conditions: E = E° - (RT/nF)ln Q.
Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different neutron counts, affecting atomic mass but not chemical properties.
Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)
Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding networks between molecules
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: Industrial process design using Hess's Law to predict energy requirements for multi-step chemical syntheses without performing every reaction experimentally
Medical PET scans using positron-emitting isotopes (¹⁸F-FDG) to image metabolic activity and detect cancers
Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding networks between molecules
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Mass spectrometry separating isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio to determine elemental composition in forensics and pharmaceutical analysis
Gecko adhesion: billions of tiny setae on gecko feet exploit London dispersion forces to adhere to surfaces, inspiring synthetic 'gecko tape' technologies
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes
Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles?

options.text: Enzyme inhibitors as drugs: statins lower cholesterol by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase, effectively raising the Ea for cholesterol synthesis
Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Enzyme catalysis lowering Ea for biochemical reactions, allowing them to proceed rapidly at body temperature (37°C) rather than requiring extreme heating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles in practice?

options.text: Breathalyzer devices using electrochemical oxidation of ethanol to acetic acid, generating a current proportional to blood alcohol concentration
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes
Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste
Medical PET scans using positron-emitting isotopes (¹⁸F-FDG) to image metabolic activity and detect cancers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Aufbau principle"?

options.text: A species produced in one elementary step and consumed in a subsequent step; does not appear in the overall equation
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest
A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu

quiz_questions.explanation: "Aufbau principle" is defined as: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Pauli exclusion principle"?

options.text: The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
No two electrons in the same atom can have identical sets of four quantum numbers; each orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins
Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pauli exclusion principle" is defined as: No two electrons in the same atom can have identical sets of four quantum numbers; each orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hund's rule"?

options.text: Electrons occupy degenerate (equal-energy) orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing up
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hund's rule" is defined as: Electrons occupy degenerate (equal-energy) orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing up. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Valence electrons"?

options.text: A species produced in one elementary step and consumed in a subsequent step; does not appear in the overall equation
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current
Electrons in the outermost energy level (highest principal quantum number n); determine chemical bonding and reactivity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Valence electrons" is defined as: Electrons in the outermost energy level (highest principal quantum number n); determine chemical bonding and reactivity. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Orbital"?

options.text: The minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state and form products; determines the rate's temperature sensitivity
A region of space where there is a high probability of finding an electron; types: s (spherical), p (dumbbell), d (cloverleaf), f (complex)
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; ΔH < 0; products are lower in energy than reactants

quiz_questions.explanation: "Orbital" is defined as: A region of space where there is a high probability of finding an electron; types: s (spherical), p (dumbbell), d (cloverleaf), f (complex). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest"?

options.text: Half-reaction method
Aufbau principle
Selective precipitation
Crystalline solid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Aufbau principle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "No two electrons in the same atom can have identical sets of four quantum numbers; each orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins"?

options.text: Ion-dipole force
Stoichiometric conversion
Diprotic acid
Pauli exclusion principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Pauli exclusion principle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electrons occupy degenerate (equal-energy) orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing up"?

options.text: Hydrogen bonding
Pressure/volume change
Hund's rule
Heterogeneous catalyst

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hund's rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electrons in the outermost energy level (highest principal quantum number n); determine chemical bonding and reactivity"?

options.text: Valence electrons
Isotope
Polarizability
ΔS prediction rules

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Valence electrons".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A region of space where there is a high probability of finding an electron; types: s (spherical), p (dumbbell), d (cloverleaf), f (complex)"?

options.text: Mole
Orbital
ΔS prediction rules
Kp vs. Kc

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Orbital".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electron Configuration is accurate?

options.text: Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.
Electron Configuration describes how electrons are arranged in atoms following the Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund's rule.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electron Configuration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electron Configuration?

options.text: The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.
Percent yield (actual/theoretical × 100%) measures reaction efficiency.
Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
Orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electron Configuration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electron Configuration?

options.text: Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.
Each orbital holds a maximum of 2 electrons with opposite spins.
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electron Configuration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electron Configuration?

options.text: Electron configurations explain periodic trends, chemical reactivity, and spectral properties.
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electron Configuration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electron Configuration?

options.text: Noble gas core notation (e.g., [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s² for Zn) simplifies writing configurations.
Rate Laws and Reaction Order describe the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations.
The autoionization of water (Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C) connects [H⁺] and [OH⁻].
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electron Configuration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electron Configuration?

options.text: Flame tests and fireworks colors: excited electrons emit characteristic wavelengths when returning to ground state (Na = yellow, Cu = green/blue, Sr = red)
Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Enzyme catalysis lowering Ea for biochemical reactions, allowing them to proceed rapidly at body temperature (37°C) rather than requiring extreme heating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electron Configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electron Configuration?

options.text: Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Semiconductor doping in electronics: adding atoms with different electron configurations (P for n-type, B for p-type) to silicon to control conductivity
Drug elimination from the body often following first-order kinetics: dosing intervals based on half-life to maintain therapeutic blood levels

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electron Configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electron Configuration applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Transition metal colors in compounds arising from d-orbital splitting: Cu²⁺ = blue, Fe³⁺ = yellow, Cr³⁺ = green
Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Greenhouse gas behavior: CO₂ (linear, no permanent dipole) still absorbs IR radiation through asymmetric stretching, while symmetric stretching is IR-inactive

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electron Configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electron Configuration?

options.text: Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes

Tempered safety glass as an amorphous solid that, when broken, shatters into small granular pieces rather than sharp shards due to balanced internal stresses from thermal treatment (distinct from laminated glass, which holds together via a...
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Flame tests and fireworks colors: excited electrons emit characteristic wavelengths when returning to ground state (Na = yellow, Cu = green/blue, Sr = red)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electron Configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electron Configuration in practice?

options.text: Soil pH affecting nutrient availability for plants: most crops grow best at pH 6.0-7.0; acid rain lowering soil pH reduces crop productivity
Semiconductor doping in electronics: adding atoms with different electron configurations (P for n-type, B for p-type) to silicon to control conductivity
Steel alloys: adding carbon (interstitial) to iron increases hardness; adding chromium and nickel (substitutional) creates stainless steel resistant to corrosion
Gold's malleability allowing it to be hammered into gold leaf just 0.1 μm thick, used in electronics connections, architectural decoration, and aerospace heat shields

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electron Configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Effective nuclear charge (Zeff)"?

options.text: An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally
The net positive charge experienced by valence electrons: Zeff = Z - S (shielding); increases across a period as protons increase but shielding stays roughly constant
The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu

quiz_questions.explanation: "Effective nuclear charge (Zeff)" is defined as: The net positive charge experienced by valence electrons: Zeff = Z - S (shielding); increases across a period as protons increase but shielding stays roughly constant. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ionization energy (IE)"?

options.text: A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
The energy required to remove the highest-energy electron from a gaseous atom; increases across a period and up a group
Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ionization energy (IE)" is defined as: The energy required to remove the highest-energy electron from a gaseous atom; increases across a period and up a group. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electronegativity"?

options.text: A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass
A measure of an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond; Pauling scale—F is the highest at 4.0
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electronegativity" is defined as: A measure of an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond; Pauling scale—F is the highest at 4.0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electron affinity"?

options.text: ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally
The energy change when a gaseous atom gains an electron; halogens have the most negative (exothermic) electron affinities
The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electron affinity" is defined as: The energy change when a gaseous atom gains an electron; halogens have the most negative (exothermic) electron affinities. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Atomic radius"?

options.text: A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass
The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron shell; decreases across a period (more Zeff), increases down a group (more shells)
The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs)
The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally

quiz_questions.explanation: "Atomic radius" is defined as: The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron shell; decreases across a period (more Zeff), increases down a group (more shells). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The net positive charge experienced by valence electrons: Zeff = Z - S (shielding); increases across a period as protons increase but shielding stays roughly constant"?

options.text: Hess's Law
Standard reduction potential (E°)
Effective nuclear charge (Zeff)
Spectator ion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Effective nuclear charge (Zeff)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy required to remove the highest-energy electron from a gaseous atom; increases across a period and up a group"?

options.text: Crystalline solid
Conjugate acid-base pair
ICE table
Ionization energy (IE)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ionization energy (IE)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A measure of an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a covalent bond; Pauling scale—F is the highest at 4.0"?

options.text: ΔG° = -nFE°
Kinetic molecular theory
Electronegativity
pKa

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electronegativity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy change when a gaseous atom gains an electron; halogens have the most negative (exothermic) electron affinities"?

options.text: Electron affinity
Equilibrium vs. completion
Stoichiometric conversion
Spectator ion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electron affinity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron shell; decreases across a period (more Zeff), increases down a group (more shells)"?

options.text: Lewis structure
Atomic radius
Aufbau principle
Ion-dipole force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Atomic radius".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Periodic Table Trends is accurate?

options.text: Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
For redox reactions, the half-reaction method balances electron transfer in acidic or basic solutions.
Periodic Table Trends arise from systematic changes in electron configuration across periods and groups.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Periodic Table Trends as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Periodic Table Trends?

options.text: Atomic radius decreases left to right (increasing Zeff) and increases top to bottom (more electron shells).
Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic), forming bonds releases energy (exothermic).
pH and pOH provide logarithmic scales for expressing H⁺ and OH⁻ concentrations. pH = -log[H⁺], pOH = -log[OH⁻], and pH + pOH = 14.00 at 25°C.
Rate Laws and Reaction Order describe the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Periodic Table Trends as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Periodic Table Trends?

options.text: Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.
Weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka/Kb values <1.
Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) and strong bases (group 1/2 hydroxides) dissociate completely.
Ionization energy and electronegativity generally increase left to right and bottom to top.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Periodic Table Trends as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Periodic Table Trends?

options.text: These trends explain reactivity patterns: alkali metals are highly reactive (low IE, lose e⁻ easily), while noble gases are inert (full valence shells, high IE).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Stoichiometry is accurate?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
A valid mechanism must: (1) have elementary steps that sum to the overall equation, (2) be consistent with the experimentally determined rate law, and (3) have each step be physically reasonable.
Equilibrium is dynamic (reactions continue but net change is zero).
Increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Stoichiometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Stoichiometry?

options.text: Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles forms the foundation of chemistry.
Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
One mole (6.022 × 10²³ particles, Avogadro's number) provides the bridge between atomic-scale masses and laboratory-scale quantities.
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Stoichiometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Stoichiometry?

options.text: K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
Molar mass (g/mol) equals the atomic or molecular mass in amu.
Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Stoichiometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Stoichiometry?

options.text: Activation energy (Ea) is the energy barrier that must be overcome for a reaction to proceed.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.
Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Stoichiometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Stoichiometry?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Alloys (mixtures of metals) modify properties by introducing different-sized atoms.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Stoichiometry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Stoichiometry?

options.text: Pharmaceutical dosing calculations using molar mass conversions to determine the exact amount of active ingredient per tablet or solution
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
The spontaneous rusting of iron: despite being exothermic (ΔH < 0), rust formation is also driven by the entropy increase from solid Fe converting to flaky Fe₂O₃

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Stoichiometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Stoichiometry?

options.text: Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Forensic analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify and quantify drugs, poisons, and accelerants in criminal investigations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Stoichiometry.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Stoichiometry applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Fluorine's extreme electronegativity making it the most reactive nonmetal, used in Teflon (PTFE) coatings and uranium enrichment (UF₆)
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Aluminum's amphoteric nature: Al(OH)₃ dissolves in both acids and bases, exploited in the Bayer process to purify alumina from bauxite ore
Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Stoichiometry.

Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Pharmaceutical dosing calculations using molar mass conversions to determine the exact amount of active ingredient per tablet or solution

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equilibrium constant (K)"?

options.text: Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C; derived from the autoionization of water: 2H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equilibrium constant (K)" is defined as: The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reaction quotient (Q)"?

options.text: The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)
Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reaction quotient (Q)" is defined as: Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dynamic equilibrium"?

options.text: The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W
ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve
A state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations remain constant but reactions continue at the molecular level

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dynamic equilibrium" is defined as: A state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations remain constant but reactions continue at the molecular level. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Kp vs. Kc"?

options.text: The number of shared electron pairs between two atoms: single (1), double (2), triple (3); higher order = shorter, stronger bond
Kp uses partial pressures for gas-phase equilibria; Kc uses molar concentrations; related by Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn
A balanced equation that includes the enthalpy change (ΔH); coefficients represent moles, and the physical state of each species must be specified
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons; same Z but different A (e.g., ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Kp vs. Kc" is defined as: Kp uses partial pressures for gas-phase equilibria; Kc uses molar concentrations; related by Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Chemical Equilibrium is accurate?

options.text: Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles forms the foundation of chemistry.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Chemical Equilibrium?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.
Equilibrium is dynamic (reactions continue but net change is zero).
Comparing Qsp to Ksp predicts precipitation: if Qsp > Ksp, a precipitate forms.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Chemical Equilibrium?

options.text: The excess reagent remains partially unreacted.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.
The equilibrium constant expression K relates product and reactant concentrations: for aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD, K = [C]ᶜ[D]ᵈ/[A]ᵃ[B]ᵇ.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Chemical Equilibrium?

K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Balancing Chemical Equations ensures the law of conservation of mass is satisfied: the same number of each type of atom appears on both sides.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Chemical Equilibrium?

options.text: K is dimensionless and temperature-dependent.

Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.
Molecular Geometry is predicted by VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion): electron groups around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Equilibrium as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Chemical Equilibrium?

options.text: Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Chemical Equilibrium?

options.text: Blood oxygen transport: hemoglobin-oxygen equilibrium (Hb + O₂ ⇌ HbO₂) shifts based on O₂ partial pressure in lungs vs. tissues (Bohr effect)
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)
Corrosion of iron (rusting): 4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ → 2Fe₂O₃·3H₂O; prevented by galvanizing (Zn coating) or painting

Hand warmers using exothermic iron oxidation (4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃) to release heat on demand for hours
Carbon dioxide equilibrium in oceans (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺) affected by rising atmospheric CO₂, causing ocean acidification
Lewis acid catalysis in organic synthesis: BF₃ and AlCl₃ as Lewis acids in Friedel-Crafts reactions for pharmaceutical manufacturing

Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)
Refrigerators and air conditioners: external work (electricity) is required to decrease entropy locally (cool the interior) while increasing total universal entropy
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chemical Equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Chemical Equilibrium in practice?

options.text: Blood oxygen transport: hemoglobin-oxygen equilibrium (Hb + O₂ ⇌ HbO₂) shifts based on O₂ partial pressure in lungs vs. tissues (Bohr effect)

Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use

Acid rain (pH < 5.6) formed when SO₂ and NOₓ from fossil fuel combustion react with water to form H₂SO₄ and HNO₃, damaging ecosystems and infrastructure
Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)

options.text: Catalytic converters using Pt, Pd, and Rh heterogeneous catalysts to lower Ea for converting exhaust pollutants (CO, NOₓ) to harmless products
Industrial production of sulfuric acid (Contact Process: 2SO₂ + O₂ → 2SO₃ → H₂SO₄), the most widely produced chemical globally (~200 million tons/year)
Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old

Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium
If a reaction is reversed, K_new = 1/K; if multiplied by n, K_new = K^n; if reactions are added, K_net = K₁ × K₂
The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W

quiz_questions.explanation: "Manipulating K" is defined as: If a reaction is reversed, K_new = 1/K; if multiplied by n, K_new = K^n; if reactions are added, K_net = K₁ × K₂. The other options describe different concepts.

This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.
Concentration expressions include molarity (M = mol/L), molality (m = mol/kg solvent), mole fraction, and mass percent.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Equilibrium Constants?

options.text: Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.
Weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka/Kb values <1.
For the reaction aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD: K = [C]ᶜ[D]ᵈ/[A]ᵃ[B]ᵇ.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Equilibrium Constants?

options.text: Lithium-ion batteries exploiting Li's small atomic radius and low ionization energy for high energy density in lightweight portable electronics
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes
Solubility predictions: Ksp of CaF₂ = 3.9 × 10⁻¹¹ indicates very low solubility, explaining why fluoride in drinking water doesn't exceed toxic levels
Haber process optimization: high pressure (fewer gas moles on product side, Δn = -2) and low temperature (exothermic) favor NH₃, but moderate T used for acceptable rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Equilibrium Constants.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Equilibrium Constants?

options.text: Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Fluorine's extreme electronegativity making it the most reactive nonmetal, used in Teflon (PTFE) coatings and uranium enrichment (UF₆)
Ammonia synthesis economics: K for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is large at low T (favors products) but reaction is too slow; industrial compromise at ~450°C, 200 atm gives usable rate and yield
Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Equilibrium Constants.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Equilibrium Constants applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Gold's malleability allowing it to be hammered into gold leaf just 0.1 μm thick, used in electronics connections, architectural decoration, and aerospace heat shields
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Equilibrium Constants.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Equilibrium Constants?

options.text: Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Solubility predictions: Ksp of CaF₂ = 3.9 × 10⁻¹¹ indicates very low solubility, explaining why fluoride in drinking water doesn't exceed toxic levels
Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Equilibrium Constants.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Equilibrium Constants in practice?

pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Le Chatelier's Principle"?

options.text: The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Le Chatelier's Principle" is defined as: When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance. The other options describe different concepts.

Equilibrium constant for weak acid dissociation: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; larger Ka = stronger acid
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry

The number of shared electron pairs between two atoms: single (1), double (2), triple (3); higher order = shorter, stronger bond
The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pressure/volume change" is defined as: Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect. The other options describe different concepts.

Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area
A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps
The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Temperature change" is defined as: The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction. The other options describe different concepts.

Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Amino acid behavior in gel electrophoresis: proteins migrate based on charge at a given pH relative to their isoelectric points
Displacement reactions in metallurgy: extracting copper by placing iron in CuSO₄ solution (Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, since Fe is more active than Cu)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Polyprotic Acids applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Phosphoric acid in food industry (E338): used in cola drinks (pH ~2.5) and as a rust remover due to its triprotic acid chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Polyprotic Acids?

options.text: Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Polyprotic Acids in practice?

options.text: Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics
Lewis acid catalysis in organic synthesis: BF₃ and AlCl₃ as Lewis acids in Friedel-Crafts reactions for pharmaceutical manufacturing
Amino acid behavior in gel electrophoresis: proteins migrate based on charge at a given pH relative to their isoelectric points
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Polyprotic Acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gibbs free energy (ΔG)"?

options.text: The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W
The energy available to do useful work; ΔG = ΔH - TΔS; negative ΔG = spontaneous
(Mass of desired product / Total mass of all products) × 100%; measures efficiency of atom utilization in a reaction
A visual representation showing the relative enthalpies of reactants, products, and intermediates; illustrates exothermic/endothermic steps

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gibbs free energy (ΔG)" is defined as: The energy available to do useful work; ΔG = ΔH - TΔS; negative ΔG = spontaneous. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Spontaneous process"?

options.text: The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)
A process that occurs without continuous external input; ΔG < 0; does NOT mean fast (kinetics is separate from thermodynamics)
A proton (H⁺) acceptor

quiz_questions.explanation: "Spontaneous process" is defined as: A process that occurs without continuous external input; ΔG < 0; does NOT mean fast (kinetics is separate from thermodynamics). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "ΔG° = -RT ln K"?

options.text: Relates standard free energy change to the equilibrium constant; ΔG°  1 (product-favored)
k = Ae^(-Ea/RT); relates the rate constant to temperature, activation energy, and the frequency/orientation factor A
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria
ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation

quiz_questions.explanation: "ΔG° = -RT ln K" is defined as: Relates standard free energy change to the equilibrium constant; ΔG°  1 (product-favored). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Temperature dependence"?

options.text: The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent
A visual representation showing the relative enthalpies of reactants, products, and intermediates; illustrates exothermic/endothermic steps
When ΔH and ΔS have the same sign, temperature determines spontaneity: T_crossover = ΔH/ΔS
A method for calculating equilibrium concentrations: Initial concentration, Change in concentration, Equilibrium concentration

quiz_questions.explanation: "Temperature dependence" is defined as: When ΔH and ΔS have the same sign, temperature determines spontaneity: T_crossover = ΔH/ΔS. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standard free energy of formation"?

options.text: ΔG°f: the free energy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states; ΔG°rxn = ΣΔG°f(products) - ΣΔG°f(reactants)
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules
A visual representation showing the relative enthalpies of reactants, products, and intermediates; illustrates exothermic/endothermic steps

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standard free energy of formation" is defined as: ΔG°f: the free energy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states; ΔG°rxn = ΣΔG°f(products) - ΣΔG°f(reactants). The other options describe different concepts.

options.text: Gibbs free energy (ΔG)
Lewis acid/base
Half-reaction method
Limiting reagent

Equilibrium vs. completion
Decomposition reaction

Brønsted-Lowry base
ΔG° = -RT ln K
Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)

Hybridization

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ΔG°f: the free energy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states; ΔG°rxn = ΣΔG°f(products) - ΣΔG°f(reactants)"?

options.text: Rate law
Standard free energy of formation
Secondary battery
Aufbau principle

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Gibbs Free Energy is accurate?

options.text: Orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...
Gibbs Free Energy (G) determines reaction spontaneity: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS.
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.
Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gibbs Free Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.
A reaction is spontaneous when ΔG  0, and at equilibrium when ΔG = 0.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gibbs Free Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: The relationship between ΔG° and K: ΔG° = -RT ln K.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
The atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons and defines the element.
Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gibbs Free Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: A valid mechanism must: (1) have elementary steps that sum to the overall equation, (2) be consistent with the experimentally determined rate law, and (3) have each step be physically reasonable.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.
Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
Reactions with ΔG°  1) are spontaneous under standard conditions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gibbs Free Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.
If a reaction is reversed, ΔH changes sign; if multiplied by a factor n, ΔH is multiplied by n.
Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gibbs Free Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gibbs Free Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: Soil pH affecting nutrient availability for plants: most crops grow best at pH 6.0-7.0; acid rain lowering soil pH reduces crop productivity
Biological coupling: ATP hydrolysis (ΔG° = -30.5 kJ/mol) drives nonspontaneous reactions like protein synthesis and active transport in cells
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Kidney dialysis using osmotic pressure principles to remove waste from blood across a semipermeable membrane

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gibbs Free Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Gibbs Free Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Rocket fuel calculations: precise stoichiometry of liquid H₂ and O₂ (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O) ensures maximum thrust without wasting expensive fuel
Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gibbs Free Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Gibbs Free Energy?

options.text: Gecko adhesion: billions of tiny setae on gecko feet exploit London dispersion forces to adhere to surfaces, inspiring synthetic 'gecko tape' technologies
Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gibbs Free Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Gibbs Free Energy in practice?

options.text: Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Biological coupling: ATP hydrolysis (ΔG° = -30.5 kJ/mol) drives nonspontaneous reactions like protein synthesis and active transport in cells
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Mass spectrometry separating isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio to determine elemental composition in forensics and pharmaceutical analysis

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Entropy (S)"?

options.text: The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
A measure of the number of possible microstates (arrangements) of a system; greater disorder = higher entropy
Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Entropy (S)" is defined as: A measure of the number of possible microstates (arrangements) of a system; greater disorder = higher entropy. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Second law of thermodynamics"?

options.text: A substance that increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower Ea; regenerated and not consumed
The entropy of the universe increases for any spontaneous process: ΔS_univ = ΔS_sys + ΔS_surr > 0
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it

quiz_questions.explanation: "Second law of thermodynamics" is defined as: The entropy of the universe increases for any spontaneous process: ΔS_univ = ΔS_sys + ΔS_surr > 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Third law of thermodynamics"?

options.text: Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect
The entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest

quiz_questions.explanation: "Third law of thermodynamics" is defined as: The entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Microstates"?

options.text: The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules
Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B (e.g., 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Microstates" is defined as: The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "ΔS prediction rules"?

options.text: Entropy increases for: solid→liquid→gas transitions, dissolution, increased temperature, increased number of moles of gas products
An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products
ΔTb = iKbm; solute particles raise the boiling point by lowering the vapor pressure; i = van't Hoff factor

quiz_questions.explanation: "ΔS prediction rules" is defined as: Entropy increases for: solid→liquid→gas transitions, dissolution, increased temperature, increased number of moles of gas products. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A measure of the number of possible microstates (arrangements) of a system; greater disorder = higher entropy"?

options.text: Entropy (S)
First law of thermodynamics
End point
Triple point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Entropy (S)".

options.text: Viscosity
Ion-dipole force
Collision theory
Second law of thermodynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Second law of thermodynamics".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero"?

options.text: Chromatography
Kinetic molecular theory
Third law of thermodynamics
Pressure/volume change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Third law of thermodynamics".

options.text: pOH
Microstates
Spectator ion
Metallic bond

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Entropy increases for: solid→liquid→gas transitions, dissolution, increased temperature, increased number of moles of gas products"?

Catalyst effect
ΔS prediction rules
Conjugate acid-base pair
